content.text: Without a deliberate ownership model, observability infrastructure degrades in a predictable pattern: every team instruments their service slightly differently, alert thresholds drift to whatever silences pages rather than what detects real problems, dashboards accumulate orphaned panels that reference metrics that no longer exist, and no one is accountable when the metrics pipeline itself falls over during an incident.

GenAI systems make this worse because they span more teams than a typical web service: the application team owns the user-facing product, the ML platform team owns training and evaluation infrastructure, the security team owns compliance and audit logging, and a cost team may own spend tracking. Each needs different views of the same telemetry, and they all depend on the same Loki/Prometheus/Tempo stack remaining healthy.
The most effective ownership structure for observability at GenAI scale is a
: a dedicated observability platform team owns the instrumentation infrastructure (collector fleet, storage backends, Grafana), and product teams are
of that platform. The platform team has explicit SLOs for the observability infrastructure itself, treats internal teams as customers, and maintains a roadmap just as any product team would.

This model decouples the reliability of the platform from the reliability of the services running on it. When a GenAI application has a latency spike at 3 AM, the on-call engineer can trust that the dashboards showing them the spike are themselves healthy — because someone else is on-call for the observability stack.

# docs/observability-platform/charter.yaml
team: observability-platform
mission: >
  Provide a reliable, self-serve telemetry platform that lets every
  engineering team understand their system's behaviour in production
  without needing to operate the underlying infrastructure.

owns:
  infrastructure:
    - OTel Collector fleet (Kubernetes DaemonSet + gateway)
    - Prometheus + Thanos (metrics storage)
    - Loki (log aggregation, all tenants)
    - Tempo (distributed traces)
    - Grafana (dashboards, alerting, RBAC)
    - Alertmanager (routing, silencing, notification channels)
  artifacts:
    - OTel SDK wrapper libraries (Python, TypeScript)
    - Dashboard template library (genai-panels.libsonnet)
    - Runbook template and CI validation scripts
    - Semantic convention governance (gen_ai.* attribute standards)

slos:
  - name: collector_ingest_availability
    target: 99.9%
    window: 30d
    description: "Spans and log lines accepted without error"
  - name: loki_query_p95_latency
    target: "<3s for 30d range queries"
    window: 7d
  - name: grafana_dashboard_load_p99
    target: "<5s"
    window: 7d

not_owns:
  - Individual service dashboards (owned by product teams via OWNERS files)
  - Alert thresholds for business SLOs (owned by product teams)
  - Instrumentation correctness within application code
  - On-call response for product-level incidents

A common mistake is treating the observability platform as a part-time responsibility for the SRE team. At the scale of a production GenAI system — ingesting millions of spans and log lines per minute — the platform requires dedicated ownership. A rough sizing heuristic: one platform engineer per 15–20 product engineering teams served.
Drives gen_ai.* convention adoption, cost observability strategy
Collector fleet, storage backends, Kubernetes ops
Tail sampling config, Loki multi-tenancy, Thanos compaction
gen_ai.* attribute instrumentation, streaming trace helpers
Grafonnet panel library, dashboard CI validation, game days
Audit log tenant, RBAC governance, PII scrubbing

Product teams should be able to onboard to the observability platform and ship their first dashboard without ever filing a ticket with the platform team. The platform team achieves this by publishing three artifacts: a self-serve SDK, a dashboard scaffolding tool, and a runbook template.

# Platform team publishes this contract; product teams fulfill it
onboarding_steps:
  1_instrument:
    what: "Add the OTel SDK wrapper to your service"
    how: "pip install genai-otel-sdk; import and configure (README in sdk/)"
    owner: product_team
    platform_provides: "SDK library, example snippets, attribute reference"

  2_dashboard:
    what: "Create your service dashboard from the standard template"
    how: "cookiecutter gh:example-org/dashboard-template"
    owner: product_team
    platform_provides: "Cookiecutter template, CI validation, panel library"

  3_alerts:
    what: "Define your SLO alert rules"
    how: "Copy alerts/genai-slo-template.yaml, fill in thresholds, open PR"
    owner: product_team
    platform_provides: "Alert template, review checklist, Alertmanager routing"

  4_runbook:
    what: "Write runbooks for your alerts"
    how: "Copy runbooks/template.md, fill in decision trees, link in alert YAML"
    owner: product_team
    platform_provides: "Runbook template, link validation CI, game day guide"

  5_review:
    what: "Platform team reviews dashboard + alert cardinality"
    how: "PR CI gates catch most issues; platform reviews label count"
    owner: platform_team
    sla: "2 business days first review"
The SDK wrapper is the most impactful artifact the platform team can ship. It encodes the

semantic conventions into a callable API, so product engineers never have to remember which span attributes to set — they call the wrapper and it handles attribution, cost calculation, and context propagation automatically.

# genai_otel_sdk/tracer.py — platform team maintains, product teams consume
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
import time

_tracer = trace.get_tracer("genai.platform.sdk", "1.3.0")

class GenAISpan:
    """Context manager that wraps a single LLM call with standard gen_ai.* attributes."""

    def __init__(self, model: str, feature: str, streaming: bool = False):
        self.model = model
        self.feature = feature
        self.streaming = streaming
        self._span = None
        self._start_ns = None

    def __enter__(self):
        self._span = _tracer.start_span(f"gen_ai.{self.feature}.completion")
        self._span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.model", self.model)
        self._span.set_attribute("gen_ai.system", "openai")
        self._span.set_attribute("gen_ai.feature", self.feature)
        self._span.set_attribute("gen_ai.streaming", self.streaming)
        self._start_ns = time.time_ns()
        return self

    def record_first_token(self):
        if self._start_ns:
            ttft_ms = (time.time_ns() - self._start_ns) / 1e6
            self._span.set_attribute("gen_ai.performance.ttft_ms", ttft_ms)

    def record_completion(self, *, prompt_tokens: int, completion_tokens: int,
                          finish_reason: str, cost_usd: float):
        self._span.set_attribute("gen_ai.usage.prompt_tokens", prompt_tokens)
        self._span.set_attribute("gen_ai.usage.completion_tokens", completion_tokens)
        self._span.set_attribute("gen_ai.usage.total_tokens", prompt_tokens + completion_tokens)
        self._span.set_attribute("gen_ai.finish_reason", finish_reason)
        self._span.set_attribute("gen_ai.cost_usd", cost_usd)

    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        if exc_type:
            self._span.set_status(trace.StatusCode.ERROR, str(exc_val))
            self._span.set_attribute("gen_ai.error.type", exc_type.__name__)
        self._span.end()
        return False


# Product team usage — no attribute knowledge required
async def my_service_handler(prompt: str):
    with GenAISpan(model="gpt-4o", feature="summarisation", streaming=True) as span:
        response = await llm_client.complete(prompt, stream=True)
        async for chunk in response:
            if chunk.is_first_token:
                span.record_first_token()
        span.record_completion(
            prompt_tokens=response.usage.prompt_tokens,
            completion_tokens=response.usage.completion_tokens,
            finish_reason=response.finish_reason,
            cost_usd=compute_cost("gpt-4o", response.usage),
        )
        return response.text
Governance: RFC Process for Platform Changes

The platform team has disproportionate blast radius: a misconfigured Loki compactor can delete months of audit logs; a collector change that introduces label cardinality can cause Prometheus to OOM across the fleet. Changes to shared infrastructure require a lightweight RFC (Request for Comments) process so product teams are not surprised.

# RFC-042: Introduce gen_ai.cost_usd span attribute

**Status:** Proposed | Under Review | Accepted | Implemented
**Author:** @platform-team-member
**Reviewers:** @genai-app-team, @ml-platform-team, @finance-team
**Decision date:** 2024-09-15

## Problem
Cost-per-request is currently computed post-hoc from token counts and a
separate pricing table. This makes cost attribution dashboards stale by ~1hr.

## Proposal
Add gen_ai.cost_usd as a float span attribute set at completion time.
Value = (prompt_tokens * input_price + completion_tokens * output_price).

## Impact on product teams
- SDK update required: genai-otel-sdk 1.3.0 (non-breaking, attribute is new)
- Existing dashboards: no change required (attribute simply not present on old spans)
- Alert rules: none affected

## Rollout plan
1. Ship SDK 1.3.0 with opt-in flag (default off, 2024-09-01)
2. Enable by default in SDK 1.4.0 (2024-10-01, 4-week notice)
3. Add gen_ai.cost_usd panel to standard dashboard template (2024-10-15)

## Rollback
Remove attribute from SDK; no storage changes needed.
The platform team's work is only valuable if product teams can ship instrumented services faster and incidents are resolved sooner. Track these leading and lagging indicators:
Fraction of services with valid OWNERS files
Weekly CI report across all dashboard repos
SDK adoption rate (% services using wrapper)
Open platform tickets older than 5 days
platform team is the only one who knows how to create alerts. Product teams cannot be self-sufficient and every change requires a ticket.
no OWNERS files, dashboards with no clear team responsible. When a panel breaks, everyone assumes someone else will fix it.
product teams file requests for every dashboard; platform team becomes a bottleneck. The platform team should ship self-service tooling, not fulfil one-off requests.
No SLOs on the platform itself:
the observability stack goes down during an incident, precisely when it is needed most, with no one accountable for platform reliability.
product teams add high-cardinality labels (user_id, request_id) to Prometheus metrics without review, causing OOM across the metrics stack.

The observability platform team owns the infrastructure (collectors, storage, Grafana) and the shared artifacts (SDK wrapper, dashboard template library, runbook tooling), while product teams own the instrumentation accuracy, alert thresholds, and dashboard content for their own services. The platform team operates like an internal product team: it has a charter, explicit SLOs, a self-service onboarding contract, and an RFC process for changes that affect the whole fleet. Effectiveness is measured by time-to-first-dashboard for new teams, SDK adoption rate, alert actionability, and the platform's own ingest availability SLO. Without this structure, the observability stack degrades into an undifferentiated pile of dashboards that nobody trusts, maintained by nobody, that fail silently during the incidents where they matter most.
